Opening

01. Chapter Identity & Learning Mission

CHAPTER 5 — DISCUSSION TEXT

Should Students Who Skip School Be Given Another Chance?

Learning to Discuss Two Sides of a School Issue with Empathy, Logic, and Responsibility

KomponenKeterangan
Mata PelajaranBahasa Inggris
Kelas/FaseKelas XI / Fase F
GenreDiscussion Text
TemaDropout, School Attendance, and Second Chances
Konteks LokalSMA Negeri 6 Pangkajene dan Kepulauan, Pulau Sapuka, Liukang Tangaya, Pangkep
Fokus IsuKehadiran sekolah, bolos, terlambat, tugas tidak selesai, risiko putus sekolah, kesempatan kedua, tanggung jawab
PendekatanGenre-Based Approach/SFL, CLT, Deep Learning, Social-Emotional Learning, Ethical Reasoning, Restorative Discipline Awareness
Produk AkhirDiscussion text 450–700 kata + fishbowl discussion + recommendation card/learning contract draft
Skill UtamaListening, Reading, Speaking, Writing, Grammar & Vocabulary in Use
Language FocusHedging language, concession, contrast connectors, modal verbs, recommendation expressions, respectful discussion language

Learning Mission

In this chapter, students learn how to discuss a difficult school issue from more than one side. The issue is not simple: some students skip school, come late, leave after the first period, do not submit assignments, or are at risk of dropping out. Some people believe they deserve another chance. Others believe school rules must be enforced firmly.

A discussion text helps students avoid one-sided thinking. It does not only ask, “Do you agree or disagree?” It asks students to consider the issue, understand arguments for and against, listen to different perspectives, and write a fair recommendation.

A fair second chance is not free from responsibility. It should come with guidance, clear conditions, and commitment to change.

Ethical and Sensitivity Note

This chapter discusses attendance problems, low motivation, missing assignments, and dropout risk as educational issues. The goal is to learn balanced discussion, not to shame any student or family.

Do not use this material to mention real student names, mock students, label students as permanently “bad,” blame families harshly, suggest that skipping school has no consequences, or turn classroom discussion into gossip.

Use respectful terms: students at risk of dropping out, students with attendance problems, students who struggle with motivation, second chance with conditions, restorative approach, and accountability.

Hook

02. Situational Hook & Learning Objectives

Situational Hook

On one hand, dropping out is often caused by factors beyond the student's control. On the other hand, school rules exist for a reason — and not everyone gets a second chance in the real world.

In many schools, teachers face a difficult question. What should they do when some students often skip school, come late, disappear after the first period, or refuse to complete assignments? Should the students be punished immediately? Should they be given another chance? Or should the school combine support with clear consequences?

In an island school like SMA Negeri 6 Pangkajene dan Kepulauan, the issue can be more complex. Some students may help their families. Some may join sea activities, help with fishing boats, or support seaweed work. Some may feel tired because of family routines. Some may lose motivation because they do not clearly see how formal education connects to life after SMA. Some may think that working at sea gives direct results while school feels far from real life.

However, school rules also matter. Students who attend regularly, complete assignments, and try their best deserve fairness. Teachers need evidence of learning. Parents need to know whether their children are really learning. A school cannot help students if repeated absence is ignored.

The deeper question is: What is fair: giving another chance, applying strict rules, or combining support with responsibility?

Tujuan Pembelajaran

Setelah mempelajari chapter ini, peserta didik mampu:

  1. memahami fungsi sosial discussion text;
  2. mengidentifikasi struktur discussion text: issue, pros, cons, dan recommendation;
  3. membedakan discussion text dari exposition text;
  4. menemukan posisi/stance penulis atau pembicara dalam teks;
  5. mengidentifikasi argumen pro dan kontra dalam teks;
  6. memahami isu putus sekolah, bolos, malas belajar, dan kesempatan kedua dari beberapa perspektif;
  7. menyampaikan pendapat dengan hedging language seperti it is argued, some believe, it seems that, dan it may be true that;
  8. menggunakan concession seperti although, even though, despite, dan while it is true that;
  9. menggunakan contrast connectors seperti on the other hand, however, in contrast, dan nevertheless;
  10. menggunakan modal verbs seperti should, might, could, must, dan need to untuk memberi saran;
  11. mendengarkan argumen lawan dan merespons secara sopan;
  12. mengikuti fishbowl discussion dengan aturan diskusi yang sehat;
  13. menulis discussion text seimbang dengan rekomendasi yang jelas;
  14. merefleksikan hubungan antara pendidikan formal, kehidupan pulau, tanggung jawab pribadi, dan masa depan siswa.

Big Questions

  1. Should students who skip school be given another chance?
  2. What makes a second chance fair?
  3. When does support become too soft?
  4. When does punishment become too harsh?
  5. Why do some students lose motivation to study?
  6. Is formal education relevant for island students?
  7. How can school rules protect learning?
  8. What responsibilities should students show after receiving another chance?
  9. How can teachers, parents, and friends help without ignoring discipline?
  10. How can discussion text help us think fairly about school problems?
BKoF

03. BKoF: Building Knowledge of the Field

Building Knowledge of the Field

Picture Prediction

Bayangkan sebuah gambar kelas di sekolah kepulauan. Seorang guru, orang tua, siswa, dan teman sebaya duduk dalam lingkaran kecil. Di papan tulis tertulis: Second Chance or Strict Consequence? Di sudut lain, terlihat tas sekolah, buku tugas yang belum selesai, dan gambar perahu di dekat dermaga.

Diskusikan: What issue is being discussed? Who should be involved? Why might a student skip school? Why might teachers still need strict rules? What does a fair second chance look like?

Issue Mapping

ProblemPossible CausesPossible EffectsPossible Responses
A student often comes late.family routine, sleep habit, distancemisses opening instructionsreminder, attendance target, parent communication
A student leaves after the first period.low motivation, peer influence, avoidancemisses learning and practicementoring, monitoring, consequence
A student does not submit assignments.confusion, laziness, no routineweak learning evidenceremedial task, deadline, teacher support
A student joins sea work and misses class.family responsibility, economic pressurefatigue, absence, dropout riskflexible support, learning contract
A student has low motivation.unclear future goal, feeling left behinddisengagementgoal-setting, peer support, counseling
A student is at risk of dropping out.repeated absence, shame, family pressureschool leavinghome visit, mentor, structured second chance

Agree/Disagree Line

Use the positions Agree, Not Sure, or Disagree.

StatementMy PositionMy Reason
Students who skip school should always be punished.
A second chance should be given only once.
Students with family duties need flexible support.
Students who do not submit assignments should not join final exams.
Formal education is still important for island students.
Friends should remind classmates who often skip lessons.
School rules should be firm but still humane.

Perspective Cards

PerspectiveMain ConcernPossible View
Studentsupport and dignity“I need help, but I must change.”
Teacherlearning evidence and fairness“I want to help, but progress must be visible.”
Parentchild’s future and family situation“School and family must communicate.”
Peer/Friendfriendship and fairness“I care, but silence can make the problem worse.”
Schoolsafe, fair, structured support“Second chances need clear conditions.”

Misconception Check

StatementTrue / False / Needs DiscussionReason
Every student who skips school is lazy.
A second chance means no consequence.
Strict rules always solve attendance problems.
Guidance without student commitment may fail.
School attendance is connected to learning evidence.
Formal education can still be relevant for island students.
Hear

04. Listening: Panel Discussion

HEAR — Listening / Panel Discussion

Title: Should They Get Another Chance?

Pre-Listening Questions

  1. What does “another chance” mean in school?
  2. Who should be involved when a student often skips school?
  3. What reasons might support giving another chance?
  4. What reasons might support strict consequences?
  5. What should happen if a student receives another chance but does not change?

Panel Discussion Script

Moderator: Good morning, everyone. Today we will discuss an important question: should students who skip school, come late, or do not complete assignments be given another chance? This issue is not simple. Some people believe that students need support, while others argue that school rules must be firm. Let us listen to different perspectives.

Teacher: From a teacher’s perspective, I believe that students should not be judged too quickly. In some cases, repeated absence may be caused by family duties, low motivation, or personal problems. However, if a student often misses class and does not submit assignments, it becomes difficult to see learning progress. A second chance might be helpful, but it should include clear targets, such as better attendance and completed remedial tasks.

Parent: As a parent, I think students deserve guidance. Island life can be demanding. Some children help their families, join sea-related activities, or feel tired because of home responsibilities. It may be true that some students lose motivation because they do not see a clear future after SMA. However, parents also need to support school rules. If a child is given another chance, the family should communicate with the school and help the child become more responsible.

Student Representative: I understand both sides. Some students are not simply lazy. They may feel confused, ashamed, or left behind after missing many lessons. If the school only punishes them, they may feel that returning is impossible. On the other hand, students must also be responsible. If we receive another chance, we should attend class, complete missed assignments, and show real effort.

Peer/Friend: As a friend, I feel empathy, but fairness matters. Many students come to school regularly, even when they are tired. They complete assignments and follow classroom rules. If students who skip school are treated as if nothing happened, it may feel unfair. Nevertheless, friends can help by reminding, inviting, and encouraging classmates to return to class instead of mocking them.

School Counselor / Vice Principal: A balanced solution is needed. A second chance should not mean no consequence. It should be a structured chance. The school could create a learning contract, contact parents, set attendance targets, give remedial tasks, and provide mentoring. If the student shows commitment, the school should support the change. However, if there is no improvement after repeated guidance, further consequences may be necessary.

Moderator: Thank you. It seems that most speakers agree on a balanced recommendation: students who skip school may be given another chance, but the chance should come with clear conditions, family communication, guidance, and responsibility.

Listening Tasks

SpeakerSupports Second Chance?Supports Consequences?Main Reason
Teacher
Parent
Student Representative
Peer/Friend
Counselor/Vice Principal

Find these expressions: in some cases, it may be true that, some people believe, a second chance might be helpful, it seems that, however, on the other hand, nevertheless.

Post-listening: Which speaker gives the most balanced argument? What condition should be included in a fair second chance?

Concept

05. Understanding Discussion Text

Understanding Discussion Text

Discussion text is used to discuss an issue from two sides. It presents arguments for and against an issue before giving a recommendation or conclusion.

The social function is to discuss an issue from different perspectives, present arguments for and against, compare ideas fairly, help readers think critically, and give a recommendation after considering both sides.

Discussion Text vs Other Texts

Text TypePurposeStructureNumber of SidesExample Topic
Discussion TextDiscusses an issue from two sidesIssue → Pros → Cons → RecommendationTwo sidesShould students who skip school get another chance?
Analytical ExpositionPersuades readers that something is true/importantThesis → Arguments → ReiterationMostly one sideStudents should be given another chance.
Hortatory ExpositionPersuades readers to do somethingThesis → Arguments → RecommendationMostly one sideSchools should guide students with attendance problems.
DebateOral argument between opposing sidesOpening → Arguments → Rebuttal → ClosingOpposing sidesStrict punishment vs second chance
Personal OpinionExpresses personal viewOpinion → ReasonOne personal sideI think students need support.

Text Structure

StructureFunctionGuiding QuestionsExample
IssueIntroduces the problem being discussedWhat is the issue? Why does it matter?Many schools face a difficult question: should students who repeatedly skip school be given another chance?
Arguments For / ProsPresents reasons supporting the issueWhy do some people agree?Some believe students should be given another chance because absence may be caused by family problems.
Arguments Against / ConsPresents reasons against the issueWhy do some people disagree?On the other hand, repeated absence is unfair to students who attend regularly.
Recommendation / ConclusionGives a balanced solution or final positionWhat should be done after considering both sides?Students may be given another chance, but it should come with clear conditions.

Common Mistake

Weak one-sided writing: “Students who skip school must be punished. They are wrong. They do not deserve another chance.”

Better discussion writing: “Some people argue that strict consequences are necessary because repeated absence harms learning and fairness. However, others believe that students may need guidance because absence can be caused by deeper problems.”

See

06. Main Model Text

Model Text 1 — Should Students Who Skip School Be Given Another Chance?

Many schools face a difficult question: should students who skip school, come late, disappear after the first period, or refuse to complete assignments be given another chance? This issue is important because attendance is connected to learning. When students are often absent, they miss explanations, classroom discussions, practice, feedback, and assessment. However, the problem is not always simple. In an island school such as SMA Negeri 6 Pangkajene dan Kepulauan in Sapuka, students may face different challenges from students in big cities. Therefore, the issue should be discussed from both sides before a fair recommendation is made.

On one hand, some people believe that students with attendance problems should be given another chance. It is argued that repeated absence is sometimes caused by factors beyond the student’s full control. Some students may have family responsibilities. Some may help with sea-related work, fishing boats, or seaweed activities. Others may feel tired, confused, or unmotivated because they do not clearly see how formal education connects to their future. In some cases, students may feel left behind after missing several lessons, and this feeling can make them avoid school even more.

Supporters of second chances also believe that punishment alone may not solve the real problem. If a student is at risk of dropping out, a harsh response might push the student farther away from school. A guided second chance, however, could help the student return to learning. For example, the school could meet the student and parents, identify the main problem, create a learning contract, give remedial tasks, and provide mentoring. This kind of support may help the student feel that returning is still possible.

Another argument for second chances is that education should open doors, not close them too quickly. For island students, school can provide more than certificates. It can strengthen communication skills, English ability, digital literacy, problem solving, and confidence. Even if a student later works in fishing, seaweed, trade, tourism, or another local field, education can still help them make better decisions and communicate with wider communities. Therefore, it may be unfair to give up on students too early.

On the other hand, others argue that school rules exist for a reason. Repeated absence is not only a personal problem; it affects teachers, classmates, and the learning process. Teachers prepare lessons and assessments, but they cannot measure progress if students do not attend class or submit assignments. Classmates who attend regularly may feel that the rules are unfair if students who skip school receive the same treatment without consequences. In this view, giving another chance without clear responsibility can weaken discipline.

Another argument against unlimited second chances is that students must learn accountability. The real world does not always give unlimited opportunities. If someone repeatedly misses work, ignores responsibilities, or breaks agreements, there may be serious consequences. School should help students prepare for adult life, and that preparation includes responsibility, time management, and commitment. Therefore, some people believe that students who skip school should face clear consequences before they are allowed to continue as usual.

There is also a concern that second chances can become ineffective if they are not structured. If a student is only told, “Please change,” without a plan, the same problem may happen again. If the school gives repeated chances but never checks attendance, assignments, or behavior, the student may not understand the seriousness of the issue. In this case, kindness can become unclear and discipline can become weak.

A balanced recommendation is needed. Students who skip school or do not complete assignments should be given another chance, but the chance should come with clear conditions. First, the student should meet with the teacher, parent, or school counselor to discuss the problem honestly. Second, the student should sign a learning contract with specific targets, such as attending class regularly, coming on time, and completing missed assignments. Third, the school should provide guidance or mentoring, especially if the student has family, emotional, or motivational difficulties. Fourth, parents should be involved so the student receives support at home. Finally, if the student does not show improvement after repeated guidance, fair consequences should follow.

In conclusion, students who skip school should not be judged too quickly, but repeated absence should not be ignored. A second chance can be fair and meaningful when it combines empathy with accountability. Schools should guide students back to learning, but students must also prove that they are ready to change. In Sapuka and other island communities, education should stay connected to real life, but students also need to understand that their future depends on responsibility, effort, and commitment.

Mini Glossary

Word/PhraseArtiExample
attendancekehadiranAttendance is connected to learning.
repeated absenceketidakhadiran berulangRepeated absence affects learning progress.
at risk of dropping outberisiko putus sekolahA student at risk of dropping out needs guidance.
learning contractkontrak belajarA learning contract can set clear targets.
accountabilitytanggung jawabStudents must learn accountability.
commitmentkomitmenChange needs real commitment.

Comprehension & Analysis

  1. What issue is discussed in the text?
  2. Why is attendance connected to learning?
  3. What local Sapuka contexts are mentioned?
  4. Why do some people support giving another chance?
  5. Why do others argue that rules must be firm?
  6. What conditions are recommended for a second chance?
  7. What does “empathy with accountability” mean?
ParagraphStructureMain Idea
1IssueThe school faces a difficult question about second chances.
2–4ProsAbsence may have deeper causes, and guidance can help students return.
5–7ConsSchool rules protect fairness, evidence of learning, and responsibility.
8RecommendationA second chance should come with conditions.
9ConclusionEmpathy and accountability must work together.
Reading

07. Core Model Discussion Texts

Core Model Discussion Texts

Model Text 2 — Should Students Who Often Come Late Receive the Same Consequences as Students Who Skip All Day?

Coming late and skipping school are both attendance problems, but they may not always have the same level of seriousness. Some people believe that students who often come late should receive the same consequences as students who skip all day. Others argue that the consequences should be different because the problems are not exactly the same.

On one hand, students who often come late still disturb the learning process. When a student enters class after the lesson has started, the teacher may need to repeat instructions. The student may miss the opening explanation and then struggle to follow the activity. If lateness happens repeatedly, it can become a habit. Some people believe that repeated lateness shows a lack of discipline, so it should receive serious consequences.

On the other hand, coming late is not always the same as skipping all day. Some students may come late because of family routines, weather, distance, or responsibilities at home. In an island context, students may also be affected by sea activities, rain, rob, or transportation issues. Although lateness should not be ignored, it may need investigation before consequences are given.

A balanced recommendation is that repeated lateness should have consequences, but the consequences should be proportional. Students who come late should explain the reason, complete missed work, and improve their punctuality. However, students who skip all day may need a stronger response because they miss more learning time.

Model Text 3 — Should Students Who Do Not Submit Assignments Be Allowed to Join Final Exams?

Many schools require students to complete assignments before joining final exams. However, some students do not submit assignments for days or weeks. This raises a difficult question: should students who do not complete assignments be allowed to join final exams?

Some people believe that students should still be allowed to join final exams. It is argued that exams can give students a chance to show what they know. Some students may not submit assignments because they feel confused, lack learning support, or have family responsibilities. If they are blocked from exams immediately, they may feel that school has already closed the door for them.

On the other hand, assignments are part of the learning process. They are not only paperwork. Assignments show whether students practice, understand, and complete learning steps. If students do not submit assignments but still join exams without conditions, it may be unfair to students who worked consistently. It may also send the message that daily effort is not important.

A balanced recommendation is that students who do not submit assignments may join final exams only after completing minimum requirements. They should complete essential remedial tasks, meet the teacher, and make a clear plan. Therefore, the school can give another chance while still protecting fairness and learning evidence.

Model Text 4 — Is Formal Education Still Relevant for Island Students?

Some students in island communities may wonder whether formal education is still relevant. They may see family members working at sea, joining fishing activities, or earning money through local work. Because of this, some students may ask: is school really useful for island life?

On one hand, some people believe that formal education may feel less relevant if lessons are disconnected from local reality. If students only learn examples from big cities, they may not see the connection to Sapuka, fishing families, seaweed work, boat travel, or island problems. Some students may think that working at sea gives more immediate results than studying in class. It seems that motivation can decrease when students do not see a clear purpose.

On the other hand, formal education can still be very important for island students. School can help students build reading, writing, communication, numeracy, digital literacy, and problem-solving skills. English can help students access information, communicate with visitors, understand technology, or explain local issues to wider audiences. Education can also help students make safer, smarter, and more responsible decisions in island life.

A balanced recommendation is that formal education remains relevant, but schools should connect learning with island life. Lessons should include local examples, real problems, practical skills, and future pathways. School is not separate from life; it can help students protect and improve life on the island.

Model Text 5 — Should Schools Use Punishment or Guidance for Students with Attendance Problems?

Attendance problems can be handled in different ways. Some schools use punishment to stop repeated absence. Others prefer guidance to help students understand and change. The question is: should schools use punishment or guidance for students with attendance problems?

Some people believe punishment is necessary. Repeated absence is a serious problem because it affects learning, classroom routines, and fairness. If students skip lessons without consequences, they may think school rules are not important. Punishment can show that attendance matters and that every student is expected to follow school agreements.

However, others argue that punishment alone may not solve the problem. Students with attendance problems may be facing family pressure, low motivation, emotional difficulty, peer influence, or confusion about school goals. If the school only punishes them, the real cause may remain unsolved. Guidance can help the school understand what is happening and help students return to learning.

A balanced recommendation is that schools should use both guidance and consequences. Guidance should come first when the school needs to understand the cause. However, if the student refuses to improve after repeated support, consequences should follow. In this way, the school remains humane but also firm.

Model Text 6 — Should Friends Help or Report Classmates Who Often Skip Lessons?

Friendship can become complicated when a classmate often skips lessons. Some students may want to protect their friend by staying silent. Others may think that reporting the problem is more responsible. Should friends help or report classmates who often skip lessons?

On one hand, friends should help because they may understand the student’s situation better than adults. A friend can ask kindly, invite the student back to class, share missed notes, or encourage the student to talk to a teacher. Sometimes, a student who skips lessons may feel ashamed or afraid. A caring friend can make returning to class feel easier.

On the other hand, staying silent can become a problem. If a friend knows that a classmate often skips lessons but never tells anyone, the problem may become worse. The student may miss more lessons, fail to complete assignments, and become closer to dropping out. In this case, silence is not true friendship. It may allow the problem to continue.

A balanced recommendation is that friends should first remind and encourage the classmate kindly. If the problem continues, they should ask a trusted adult for help. Friends should not mock, expose, or shame the student. Real friendship means caring enough to help someone return to learning.

Core Text Tasks

For each text, identify the issue, arguments for, arguments against, recommendation, hedging language, modal verbs, and local connection. Then discuss: Which recommendation is most realistic for your school?

Lab

08. Stance and Argument Lab

Stance and Argument Lab

TermMeaningExample
IssueThe problem being discussedShould students who skip school get another chance?
StanceA position or viewpointThey should get another chance with conditions.
ReasonWhy someone holds a stanceSome students face problems beyond their control.
Evidence/ExampleSupport for a reasonSome students help their families at sea.
CounterargumentArgument from the other sideHowever, repeated absence is unfair to other students.
RecommendationFinal suggestionSchools should combine support with clear consequences.

Identify the Stance

SentenceStance
Students should receive guidance because absence may have deeper causes.
Repeated absence should have clear consequences.
A second chance should be given only with a learning contract.
Schools should not ignore missing assignments.
Friends should remind classmates before reporting to teachers.

Turn Emotional Opinion into Balanced Argument

Emotional OpinionBalanced Argument
Students who skip school are just lazy.In some cases, students may skip school because of low motivation, but repeated absence still needs responsibility.
Teachers are too strict.Some students may feel rules are strict; however, rules can protect learning and fairness.
Rules do not matter.While guidance is important, school rules still help students learn responsibility.
Second chances are useless.A second chance may fail without structure, but it can work with clear targets and mentoring.
Friends should never report friends.Friends should remind kindly first, but they may need adult help if the problem continues.

Add a Counterargument

  1. Some people believe students should get another chance because they may face family problems. However, ________________________________.
  2. It is argued that strict consequences protect fairness. On the other hand, ________________________________.
  3. Some students may work at sea to help their families. Nevertheless, ________________________________.
  4. Guidance can help students return to school. However, ________________________________.
  5. Friends should care about classmates. Even so, ________________________________.

Write a Recommendation

Use the pattern: Therefore, students should/may/could __________, but __________.

Examples: Therefore, students may be given another chance, but they must complete a learning contract. Therefore, schools should provide guidance, but consequences must remain clear.

Grammar

09. Grammar and Language Lab

Grammar and Language Lab

A. Hedging Language

Hedging language makes opinions sound careful, respectful, and less extreme. It is important in discussion text because the writer discusses more than one side.

ExpressionArtiFunction
Some people believe that...Sebagian orang percaya bahwa...Introduces a view
It is argued that...Dikatakan bahwa...Presents a formal argument
It seems that...Tampaknya...Gives careful interpretation
It may be true that...Mungkin benar bahwa...Admits possibility
In some cases,...Dalam beberapa kasus,...Avoids overgeneralization
It is possible that...Ada kemungkinan bahwa...Shows caution

Examples: Some people believe that students who skip school should be guided, not only punished. It is argued that a second chance can help students return to learning. It seems that low motivation is sometimes connected to unclear future goals. In some cases, students miss school because of family responsibilities.

B. Concession

Concession helps writers admit that the other side has a valid point before presenting another view.

ConnectorArtiExample
althoughmeskipunAlthough students need support, they must show responsibility.
even thoughmeskipunEven though island life can be difficult, attendance is still important.
despitemeskipunDespite family duties, students need to communicate with teachers.
while it is true thatmeskipun benar bahwaWhile it is true that rules are important, punishment alone may not solve the problem.

Common mistake: Although students need support, but they must be responsible. Correct: Although students need support, they must be responsible.

C. Contrast Connectors

ConnectorArtiExample
on the other handdi sisi lainOn the other hand, repeated absence can be unfair to other students.
howevernamunHowever, giving another chance without conditions may not be effective.
in contrastsebaliknyaIn contrast, a guided second chance can help students change.
neverthelessmeskipun demikianNevertheless, school rules must remain clear.
yetnamunStudents need support, yet they must also be responsible.

D. Modal Verbs

ModalFunctionExample
shouldadvice/recommendationStudents should complete missed assignments.
mightpossibilityA student might need guidance.
couldpossible solutionSchools could provide mentoring.
muststrong obligationStudents must show responsibility.
need tonecessityParents need to communicate with teachers.
have toobligationStudents have to follow school rules.

Common mistakes: Students should to attend class → Students should attend class. Students must completed assignments → Students must complete assignments.

E. Recommendation Sentences

PatternExample
Therefore, + recommendationTherefore, students should be given another chance with clear responsibilities.
It is recommended that + clauseIt is recommended that schools combine support and consequences.
A balanced solution would be + noun phraseA balanced solution would be a learning contract, mentoring, and parental communication.
Schools should + verbSchools should provide guidance but keep consequences clear.
Students need to + verbStudents need to show commitment after receiving another chance.

Practice

  1. Add hedging: “Students who skip school do not care about education.”
  2. Complete: Although students need a second chance, __________.
  3. Choose: Students need support. __________, they must show responsibility. (However / Because)
  4. Complete: Schools __________ provide mentoring. (could / to)
  5. Write: A balanced solution would be ____________________.
Speaking Language

10. Hedging & Respectful Discussion Language

Respectful Discussion Language

Discussion should be polite. Students can disagree without attacking others.

ExpressionFunction
I understand your point, but...Disagree politely
I partly agree because...Agree partly
That may be true; however...Give contrast
Could you explain your reason?Ask for clarification
I see it differently because...Present another view
I agree with the idea that...Support an argument
I respect your opinion, but...Disagree respectfully
From a student’s perspective,...Show perspective
From a teacher’s perspective,...Show perspective
A balanced solution might be...Offer recommendation

Mini Dialogue

Student A: I think students who skip school should be punished immediately.
Student B: I understand your point, but some students may have serious problems outside school.
Student A: That may be true. However, repeated absence is unfair to students who attend regularly.
Student B: I partly agree. A second chance should not mean no consequence.
Student A: So what is a fair solution?
Student B: A balanced solution might be a learning contract, parent communication, and clear attendance targets.

Mini Speaking Practice

Complete and say the sentences.

  1. Some people believe that students who skip school should __________.
  2. It is argued that school rules are important because __________.
  3. Although students need support, they must __________.
  4. On the other hand, repeated absence can __________.
  5. Therefore, a fair second chance should __________.
Vocabulary

11. Vocabulary in Use

Vocabulary in Use

Vocabulary is not only a word list. Use it for listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

School Attendance Vocabulary

EnglishArtiExample
attendancekehadiranAttendance is important for learning.
absenceketidakhadiranRepeated absence affects progress.
repeated absenceketidakhadiran berulangRepeated absence should not be ignored.
skip schoolbolos sekolahSome students skip school for different reasons.
come latedatang terlambatStudents who come late miss instructions.
disappear from classmenghilang dari kelasSome students disappear after the first period.
attend regularlyhadir secara teraturStudents should attend regularly.
school rulesaturan sekolahSchool rules protect learning.
first periodjam pelajaran pertamaSome students attend the first period only.
learning timewaktu belajarAbsence reduces learning time.

Learning Responsibility Vocabulary

EnglishArtiExample
assignmenttugasAssignments show learning progress.
deadlinebatas waktuStudents should respect deadlines.
submitmengumpulkanSubmit your assignment on time.
completemenyelesaikanStudents must complete missed tasks.
remedial tasktugas perbaikanA remedial task can help students catch up.
learning contractkontrak belajarA learning contract sets clear targets.
commitmentkomitmenChange needs commitment.
progresskemajuanTeachers need to see progress.
responsibilitytanggung jawabStudents must show responsibility.
monitoringpemantauanMonitoring helps check improvement.

Dropout and Motivation Vocabulary

EnglishArtiExample
dropoutputus sekolahDropout can affect a student’s future.
dropout riskrisiko putus sekolahStudents with dropout risk need support.
low motivationmotivasi rendahLow motivation may cause absence.
future goaltujuan masa depanStudents need clearer future goals.
family responsibilitytanggung jawab keluargaFamily responsibility can affect attendance.
peer influencepengaruh teman sebayaPeer influence can affect choices.
economic pressuretekanan ekonomiEconomic pressure may influence attendance.
guidancebimbinganGuidance can help students return.
mentoringpendampinganMentoring supports students at risk.
left behindtertinggalStudents may feel left behind after missing lessons.

Discussion and Argument Vocabulary

EnglishArtiExample
issueisu/masalahThe issue is second chances.
argumentargumenA discussion text presents arguments.
reasonalasanGive a clear reason.
evidencebuktiUse evidence or examples.
counterargumentargumen lawanAdd a counterargument.
perspectivesudut pandangConsider different perspectives.
recommendationrekomendasiEnd with a recommendation.
conclusionkesimpulanThe conclusion should be balanced.
stanceposisi pendapatIdentify the writer’s stance.
pro / conmendukung / menolakDiscussion texts include pro and con sides.

Local Sapuka Context Vocabulary

EnglishArtiExample
island studentsiswa pulauIsland students may face different challenges.
fishing familykeluarga nelayanA fishing family may depend on sea work.
sea workpekerjaan lautSome students help with sea work.
seaweed activityaktivitas rumput lautSeaweed activity can be part of family work.
boat schedulejadwal kapalBoat schedules can affect routines.
family dutytugas keluargaFamily duty may affect attendance.
formal educationpendidikan formalFormal education can still be relevant.
local livelihoodmata pencaharian lokalLocal livelihood is connected to the sea.

Phrase Chunks

Introducing the issue: The issue is whether... / Many schools face a difficult question... / This issue is important because...

Presenting arguments for: Some people believe that... / It is argued that... / In some cases...

Presenting arguments against: On the other hand... / However, others argue that... / A concern is that...

Giving recommendation: Therefore... / It is recommended that... / A balanced solution would be...

Practice

12. Reading and Viewing Practice

Reading and Viewing Practice

Identify the Issue

TitleIssue
Should Students Who Skip School Be Given Another Chance?
Should Students Who Often Come Late Receive the Same Consequences as Students Who Skip All Day?
Should Students Who Do Not Submit Assignments Be Allowed to Join Final Exams?
Is Formal Education Still Relevant for Island Students?
Should Schools Use Punishment or Guidance for Students with Attendance Problems?
Should Friends Help or Report Classmates Who Often Skip Lessons?

Identify Pros, Cons, and Recommendation

SentencePros / Cons / Recommendation
Some students may skip school because of family responsibilities.
Repeated absence can be unfair to students who attend regularly.
Schools should give another chance with clear conditions.
Punishment alone may not solve dropout risk.
Students must complete missed assignments.
A learning contract could help students change.

Distinguish Discussion from Exposition

Text ExcerptDiscussion / ExpositionReason
Students who skip school should be punished because discipline is important.
Some people support second chances, while others argue that strict consequences are necessary.
I strongly believe that all students must attend school every day.
Although students may have family duties, repeated absence still needs clear consequences.

Revise One-Sided Paragraph into Balanced Discussion

One-sided paragraph: Students who skip school are wrong and should be punished. They do not care about learning. If they do not submit assignments, they should not get any chance. School rules must be strict.

Rewrite it with one hedging expression, one argument for guidance, one argument for consequences, and one balanced recommendation.

Say

13. Speaking: Fishbowl Discussion

Speaking — Fishbowl Discussion

Fishbowl discussion is a speaking activity where a small group discusses in the middle while the rest of the class observes. It helps students practice listening, speaking, responding politely, and thinking from different perspectives.

Topic

Should students who skip school be given another chance?

Roles in the Inner Circle

  1. Student Perspective
  2. Teacher Perspective
  3. Parent Perspective
  4. Peer/Friend Perspective

Fishbowl Rules

  1. Speak respectfully.
  2. Do not mention real student names.
  3. Do not mock anyone.
  4. Listen before responding.
  5. Use evidence or examples, not gossip.
  6. Use hedging language.
  7. Respond to ideas, not to people personally.
  8. Try to give a balanced recommendation.

Role Cards

Student Perspective: Some students may have problems that others do not see. A second chance might help them return to learning. However, students must complete missed assignments.

Teacher Perspective: I understand that students may have difficulties. However, repeated absence affects learning progress. Teachers need evidence from assignments and participation.

Parent Perspective: In some cases, students have family responsibilities. Parents need to communicate with teachers. School and family should work together.

Peer/Friend Perspective: I want to help my friend, not shame them. On the other hand, it can be unfair to students who attend regularly.

Observer Sheet

Item ObservedNotes
Strong argument for second chance
Strong argument for consequences
Hedging language used
Contrast/concession used
Modal verbs used
Respectful response
Final recommendation

One-Minute Balanced Opinion Template

Good morning, everyone. Today, I will discuss whether __________. Some people believe that __________. This is because __________. On the other hand, __________. Although __________, __________. Therefore, I believe that __________. Thank you.

Writing

14. Writing Workshop

Writing Workshop

Do not write the long text immediately. Build it step by step.

Step 1 — Choose an Issue

Choose one: Should students who skip school be given another chance? Should students who often come late receive strict consequences? Should students who do not submit assignments join final exams? Is formal education still relevant for island students? Should schools use punishment or guidance for attendance problems? Should friends help or report classmates who often skip lessons? Should students who join sea work be given flexible support? Should second chances require a learning contract?

Step 2 — Write the Issue Statement

Pattern: Many schools face a difficult question: should __________?

Step 3 — List Arguments For

Argument ForReasonExample

Step 4 — List Arguments Against

Argument AgainstReasonExample

Step 5 — Add Language

Use at least four hedging expressions, three contrast/concession connectors, and four modal verbs.

Language ToolExamples to Use
HedgingSome people believe that..., It is argued that..., In some cases..., It seems that...
ContrastOn the other hand..., However..., Nevertheless...
ConcessionAlthough..., Even though..., Despite...
Modal verbsshould, could, might, must, need to

Discussion Text Template

Title: Should __________?

Paragraph 1 — Issue: Many schools face a difficult question: should __________? This issue is important because __________. In a school like __________, the problem can be more complex because __________.

Paragraph 2 — Arguments For: Some people believe that __________. It is argued that __________. In some cases, __________. Therefore, __________.

Paragraph 3 — Arguments Against: On the other hand, others argue that __________. This is because __________. Moreover, __________. Therefore, __________.

Paragraph 4 — Recommendation: A balanced solution would be __________. Although __________, __________. Therefore, I believe that __________.

Editing Checklist

Use

15. JCoT and ICoT

JCoT — Joint Construction of Text

Theme: Should Students Who Skip School Be Given Another Chance?

QuestionClass Ideas
What is the issue?
Why do some students skip school?
Why should they get another chance?
Why should consequences still exist?
What conditions should be included?
What recommendation is fair?

Shared Discussion Text Example

Many schools face a difficult question: should students who skip school be given another chance? Some people believe they should because repeated absence may be caused by family responsibilities, low motivation, or personal problems. In some cases, a student may feel left behind and need guidance to return to learning. On the other hand, repeated absence cannot be ignored because it affects learning progress and fairness. Students who attend regularly may feel that rules are weak if absence has no consequence. Therefore, students who skip school may be given another chance, but it should come with clear conditions, such as a learning contract, parent communication, improved attendance, and completed remedial tasks.

ICoT — Independent Writing Mission

Write a discussion text about a school issue related to attendance, responsibility, or second chances.

Choose One Topic

  1. Should students who skip school be given another chance?
  2. Should students who often come late receive strict consequences?
  3. Should students who do not submit assignments join final exams?
  4. Is formal education still relevant for island students?
  5. Should schools use punishment or guidance for attendance problems?
  6. Should friends help or report classmates who often skip lessons?
  7. Should students who join sea work be given flexible support?
  8. Should second chances require a learning contract?

Length: 450–700 words or one full page.

Required: title, issue, arguments for, arguments against, recommendation, at least 4 hedging expressions, at least 3 contrast/concession connectors, at least 4 modal verbs, respectful tone, and local connection if relevant.

Project

16. LRT, Projects, and Reflection

LRT — Linking Related Texts

Panel Discussion Script

Write a short panel discussion with moderator, one speaker supporting second chance, one speaker supporting strict consequences, and one speaker giving a balanced recommendation.

Pro/Con Card

Create a card with two sides: Arguments For and Arguments Against, then add a Balanced Recommendation.

Recommendation Letter

Dear School Leaders,

I would like to give a recommendation about __________. Some people believe that __________. On the other hand, __________. After considering both sides, I recommend that __________.

Sincerely, __________

Learning Contract Draft

Student name: __________ Class: __________ Issue: __________

I agree to attend class regularly, come on time, complete missed assignments, communicate if I have a problem, meet my teacher/mentor regularly, and show progress within the agreed time.

Student signature: __________ Parent/guardian signature: __________ Teacher signature: __________

Project Options

A. Create a pro/con infographic.
B. Create a fishbowl discussion poster.
C. Create a second chance agreement card.
D. Create a school attendance campaign poster.
E. Create a panel discussion script.
F. Create a short speech: “A Fair Second Chance.”
G. Create a recommendation letter to school leaders.
H. Create a learning contract template.

Reflection

  1. Mengapa sebagian siswa kehilangan motivasi belajar?
  2. Apa dampak bolos terhadap diri sendiri, guru, kelas, dan keluarga?
  3. Kapan kesempatan kedua menjadi penting?
  4. Kapan aturan harus ditegakkan dengan tegas?
  5. Apa bentuk kesempatan kedua yang adil?
  6. Apa tanggung jawab siswa setelah diberi kesempatan kedua?
  7. Apakah pendidikan formal masih relevan bagi siswa pulau? Mengapa?
  8. How can students show responsibility after making mistakes?
  9. A balanced solution would be __________.
  10. One commitment I can start this week is __________.
Quiz

17. Interactive Quiz Draft

Interactive Quiz Draft

Multiple Choice

  1. What is the main purpose of discussion text?

a. To tell a story b. To discuss an issue from two sides c. To give steps d. To describe a place

  1. Which structure belongs to discussion text?

a. Issue → Pros → Cons → Recommendation b. Goal → Materials → Steps c. Orientation → Complication → Resolution d. General Classification → Description

  1. Which sentence uses hedging language?

a. All students who skip school are lazy. b. Some people believe that students need guidance. c. Students are wrong forever. d. Never give another chance.

  1. Which connector shows contrast?

a. however b. first c. because of d. finally

  1. Which sentence uses concession?

a. Although students need support, they must show responsibility. b. Students go to school. c. The school is near the sea. d. I have a book.

  1. Which modal verb shows strong obligation?

a. might b. must c. could d. may

  1. Which is a recommendation sentence?

a. Some students skip school. b. On the other hand, rules matter. c. Therefore, schools should combine support and consequences. d. Attendance is important.

  1. Which is an argument against unlimited second chances?

a. Some students have family responsibilities. b. Punishment alone may not solve the problem. c. Unlimited chances may weaken discipline. d. Guidance can help students return.

  1. Which expression is polite disagreement?

a. You are wrong. b. I understand your point, but... c. That is stupid. d. Stop talking.

  1. What should a fair second chance include?

a. No responsibility b. No communication c. Clear conditions and commitment d. No assignments

True/False

  1. Discussion text presents only one side.
  2. Discussion text should include arguments for and against.
  3. Hedging language makes arguments more careful.
  4. A second chance should always mean no consequence.
  5. School rules can protect fairness.
  6. Students who receive another chance should show responsibility.
  7. Fishbowl discussion requires respectful listening.
  8. It is okay to mention real student names during discussion.
  9. Modal verbs can help give recommendations.
  10. A balanced recommendation considers both sides.

Gap Filling

Complete: Discussion text discusses an issue from ______ sides. The first part is the ______. Arguments supporting one side are called ______. Arguments against are called ______. The final suggestion is called a ______. “Some people believe” is ______ language. “However” is a ______ connector. “Although” is used for ______. “Should” and “must” are ______ verbs. A second chance should come with clear ______.

Teacher

18. Assessment Rubrics and Teacher Notes

Assessment Rubrics

Discussion Text Writing

Criteria4 — Excellent3 — Good2 — Developing1 — Needs Support
Clear IssueIssue is clear, specific, and relevant.Issue is clear.Issue is general or partly unclear.Issue is missing.
Balanced Pros and ConsBoth sides are well developed and fair.Both sides are present.One side is weak.Only one side is presented.
Logical ArgumentsArguments are logical, realistic, and connected.Mostly logical.Some arguments unclear.Arguments are weak.
Hedging LanguageUses hedging naturally and correctly.Uses some hedging.Limited hedging.No hedging.
Concession and ContrastUses connectors effectively.Uses some connectors.Limited use.No clear connectors.
Modal VerbsUses modal verbs accurately.Some correct use.Limited use.No modal verbs.
RecommendationBalanced, clear, and realistic.Clear.Vague.Missing.
Respectful ToneMature, respectful, and non-judgmental.Mostly respectful.Sometimes too direct.Judgmental.

Fishbowl Discussion

Criteria4 — Excellent3 — Good2 — Developing1 — Needs Support
Clarity of OpinionClear, balanced, thoughtful.Clear.Sometimes unclear.Difficult to understand.
Listening to OthersResponds directly and respectfully.Listens and responds.Limited response.Does not listen/respond.
Polite ResponseVery respectful and careful.Mostly respectful.Sometimes too direct.Disrespectful.
Hedging LanguageUses hedging effectively.Uses some hedging.Limited use.No hedging.
RelevanceArguments are relevant.Mostly relevant.Some irrelevant parts.Not relevant.

Teacher Notes

Discuss attendance problems without shaming students. Do not mention real names. Frame the issue as a general school problem. Keep fishbowl discussion safe: no mocking, no gossip, criticize ideas not people, and use polite response language.

Empathy means trying to understand the reason behind the problem. It does not mean ignoring responsibility. A second chance should include clear conditions and monitoring.

Students should understand that returning to learning requires action: attend class regularly, complete missed assignments, communicate problems, meet targets, and accept consequences if there is no improvement.

Use Sapuka context carefully. Connect the discussion to island life, sea work, family duties, motivation, and formal education, but avoid generalizations such as “island students do not care about school.”

Closing Message

A discussion text teaches us to slow down before judging. Some problems look simple from the outside, but they may be more complex when we listen carefully. A fair second chance is not the same as forgetting the problem. It is a chance to return, repair, and grow.

A second chance is meaningful only when it becomes a first step toward responsibility.

Mini Interactive Check

Discussion text presents an issue from...
